## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two retries for the same charge arrive at the same instant — who wins?"**
**Mechanism:** both try `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` on the idempotency key; the DB serializes them, exactly one insert succeeds and proceeds to charge, the other gets the conflict and returns the winner's stored result.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "They both grab for the same ticket; the database hands it to exactly one, and the other just gets shown the first one's answer."
**💬 One-liner:** "An atomic insert-on-conflict picks one winner; the loser returns the stored result — one charge, guaranteed."

**❓ "Stripe charged the card but timed out before answering. Now what?"**
**Mechanism:** the payment goes `UNCERTAIN`; we never blind-retry. A reconciler queries the processor by our idempotency key/reference to learn the truth, then completes or fails the ledger accordingly.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We don't guess — we mark it 'unknown' and ask the bank directly whether the money actually moved before doing anything."
**💬 One-liner:** "Timeout means 'unknown, go check,' never 'try again' — reconciliation resolves it with no double-charge."

**❓ "What if the payment processor goes down completely?"**
**Mechanism:** the circuit breaker opens after N consecutive failures and we fail over new payments to a backup processor; in-flight uncertain ones are reconciled when it recovers.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We stop calling the broken one, switch new charges to a backup, and clean up the in-between cases once it's back."
**💬 One-liner:** "Circuit-break, fail over to a backup processor, reconcile the in-flight on recovery."

**❓ "Can you oversell — refund more than was captured?"**
**Mechanism:** the refundable amount is computed from the ledger (captured minus prior refunds), and a transactional constraint enforces `sum(refunds) ≤ captured` at commit, so racing refunds can't both pass.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We only ever look at our own books to decide how much is left to refund, and the database itself blocks going over."
**💬 One-liner:** "Over-refund is blocked by a transactional cap computed from the ledger, not the request."

**❓ "How do you guarantee the books always balance?"**
**Mechanism:** each money event writes balanced double-entry rows in one ACID transaction with a DB-level debits-equals-credits check; a nightly reconciler re-sums entries vs balances and against the processor's settlement file.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Every dollar is written twice, in one all-or-nothing step, and the database refuses to save anything that doesn't add up to zero."
**💬 One-liner:** "Double-entry in one ACID transaction with a sum-to-zero constraint, plus nightly reconciliation."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash sale or Black Friday spike?"**
**Mechanism:** stateless API + workers autoscale; the ~10x burst (peak ~7.7K/s) is sized for; the bounded capture queue absorbs spikes and back-pressures with 429 + Retry-After if the outbound pool saturates.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We've sized for ten-times-normal, and the queue acts as a shock absorber so a spike just queues briefly instead of crashing us."
**💬 One-liner:** "We size for a 10x burst and let the bounded capture queue absorb the spike, shedding load with 429s if needed."

**❓ "Can the same payment be created twice?"**
**Mechanism:** the `Idempotency-Key` plus a `UNIQUE` constraint on it make creation idempotent; a duplicate returns the original Payment.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "No — the ticket number on the request makes a repeat return the same payment instead of a new one."
**💬 One-liner:** "No — the idempotency key is unique-constrained, so a duplicate returns the original payment."

**❓ "How do you stay out of PCI scope / not get breached for card data?"**
**Mechanism:** we tokenize at the edge — the raw card (PAN) is exchanged for a token by a PCI-compliant vault; our services and DB only ever see the token, never the PAN, so a breach of our systems exposes no card numbers.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We never store the actual card number — we swap it for a meaningless token the moment it arrives, so there's nothing valuable to steal in our database."
**💬 One-liner:** "We tokenize at the edge and never persist a raw PAN — our DB holds tokens, not cards."

**❓ "What stops a merchant from forging an amount or another merchant's payment?"**
**Mechanism:** the merchant is identified from the API key/JWT, never the body; object-level authz checks the payment belongs to that merchant; capture/refund amounts are validated against the original authorization server-side.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We trust who you are from your key, not what you type, and we check the payment is actually yours before doing anything."
**💬 One-liner:** "Identity comes from the API key, amounts are validated server-side, and every action checks object-level ownership."

**❓ "What if Redis crashes — does idempotency break?"**
**Mechanism:** Redis is a speed layer, not the source of truth; the durable `UNIQUE` constraint in Postgres still rejects duplicate idempotency keys, so we degrade to slightly higher latency but stay exactly-once.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Idempotency still holds — the database has the final say, Redis just makes the common case fast."
**💬 One-liner:** "We fall back to the Postgres unique constraint — slower, still exactly-once."

**❓ "A merchant got the same webhook twice — is that a bug?"**
**Mechanism:** no — delivery is at-least-once by design; every event has a stable `event_id` and the merchant dedupes on it, so a duplicate is harmless.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "That's expected — we'd rather tell you twice than miss it; just ignore the repeat by its id."
**💬 One-liner:** "By design — at-least-once delivery with a stable event id; merchants dedupe."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
**Mechanism:** the sharded multi-AZ ledger Postgres fleet (~$55K/mo) dominates; the biggest lever is tiering cold ledger partitions to object storage so the hot SSD footprint stays bounded.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The money database is the pricey bit because it has to be durable and replicated; we save most by moving old records to cheap cold storage."
**💬 One-liner:** "The ledger DB is the top line; tiering old partitions to object storage is the biggest saver."

**❓ "How would you add a new payment method, like a bank transfer (ACH)?"**
**Mechanism:** the processor adapter abstracts the rail; we add a new adapter implementation and a method type; the Payment state machine and ledger are method-agnostic (debits/credits don't care how money moved).
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The core doesn't care how money moves — we just plug in a new adapter for the new rail; the ledger logic is unchanged."
**💬 One-liner:** "Add a processor adapter for the new rail — the ledger and state machine are method-agnostic."

**❓ "How do you make a single payment traceable across the async hops for debugging?"**
**Mechanism:** a trace-id is generated at the gateway and propagated through the Payment Service, into the Kafka message headers, through the capture worker and the webhook delivery — so one query reconstructs the full lifecycle.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Every payment carries one tracking number through every step, even the background ones, so we can follow it end to end."
**💬 One-liner:** "A trace-id propagates gateway → service → Kafka → worker → webhook, so one payment is fully traceable."

**❓ "Why double-entry instead of just updating a balance column?"**
**Mechanism:** double-entry makes every movement self-checking (debits must equal credits) and gives an immutable audit trail; a bare balance column has no history and can silently drift with no way to detect or audit the error.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Writing every move twice means errors are instantly visible and we have a full paper trail — a single balance number can be wrong with no one noticing."
**💬 One-liner:** "Double-entry is self-checking and auditable; a balance column drifts silently."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A payment gateway takes a merchant's charge, moves the money through an external processor, and keeps a perfectly balanced record of it. The design splits into two halves. The read and notify half is async: state changes flow onto a durable Kafka bus, and a dedicated fleet delivers signed webhooks to merchants at-least-once, retrying with backoff and dead-lettering anything that won't deliver, while merchants dedupe on a stable event id. The write half is the money path: authorize, capture, and refund flow through a Payment Service that dedupes with an idempotency key and writes a double-entry ledger in sharded Postgres, where the hardest problem is the processor that charges then times out — solved by marking the payment uncertain, passing our idempotency key to the processor so retries don't double-charge, and reconciling the true outcome instead of guessing. The two halves connect through Kafka, where every committed money event emits an event for delivery. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: money must move exactly once and the books must always balance — so when in doubt, we choose consistency and reconcile, never guess."
